content_markdown:
Object P has a mass $m_{\text{P}}$ and specific heat capacity $c_{\text{P}}$. Object Q has a mass $m_{\text{Q}}$ and specific heat capacity $c_{\text{Q}}$. The temperature of each object increases by the same amount. Which of the following gives the ratio

$$\frac{\text{thermal energy transferred to object P}}{\text{thermal energy transferred to object Q}}?$$

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram present, only text and mathematical expressions.

vibe_explanation: The thermal energy transferred to an object is given by the formula $Q = mc\Delta T$. For object P, $Q_{\text{P}} = m_{\text{P}}c_{\text{P}}\Delta T$. For object Q, $Q_{\text{Q}} = m_{\text{Q}}c_{\text{Q}}\Delta T$. Since the temperature increase $\Delta T$ is the same for both objects, it cancels out when taking the ratio $Q_{\text{P}}/Q_{\text{Q}}$, leaving $(m_{\text{P}}c_{\text{P}})/(m_{\text{Q}}c_{\text{Q}})$.
